name: NVIDIA T4
strengths: widely deployed baseline inference GPU in older cloud and edge fleets
still useful for small models and strongly quantized serving paths
good target when backward fleet compatibility matters
constraints: limited memory and bandwidth for modern long-context LLM serving
no BF16 or FP8 path

name: NVIDIA A2
strengths: low-power edge and dense server inference target
best used with compact models and aggressive quantization
constraints: tight memory and bandwidth budgets make KV-cache pressure show up quickly
not a good target for large long-context models without heavy compression

name: NVIDIA A10
strengths: common cloud inference target thanks to A10G-style deployments
solid fit for quantized LLM serving and fused attention kernels
constraints: 24 GB VRAM limits larger models and long-context decode
no FP8 path and far less bandwidth than Hopper or Blackwell datacenter GPUs

name: GeForce RTX 3090
strengths: large VRAM for a consumer card
good FP16 and BF16 tensor core throughput
strong fit for fused dequant GEMM and attention kernels
constraints: memory bandwidth is still the first wall for decode-heavy LLM serving
no native FP8 path

name: NVIDIA A30
strengths: HBM-backed Ampere card that holds up well on memory-sensitive inference
good fit for BF16 or INT8 serving when FP8 is unavailable
constraints: less raw headroom than A100, H100, or B200 for large prefill workloads
older Ampere stack means no FP8 option

name: NVIDIA A40
strengths: 48 GB VRAM makes it useful for larger single-GPU enterprise inference
passive datacenter form factor is easier to deploy than workstation SKUs
constraints: GDDR bandwidth is still a bottleneck for decode-heavy serving
no FP8 path

name: NVIDIA A100
strengths: mature high-bandwidth Ampere baseline for BF16 and FP16 inference
still a strong target for fused kernels and quantized serving paths
constraints: PCIe and SXM variants differ materially in bandwidth and behavior
no FP8 path compared with Hopper and Blackwell

name: NVIDIA RTX A6000
strengths: large 48 GB workstation target for local model development and serving
useful when you need more memory than a 3090 but remain on a desktop box
constraints: workstation thermals and deployment patterns differ from passive datacenter SKUs
no FP8 path

name: GeForce RTX 4090
strengths: very strong tensor throughput for single-GPU inference
good fit for Triton attention and fused MLP kernels
often benefits from graph capture for low-latency decode
constraints: decode remains memory-sensitive because VRAM bandwidth is far below H100/B200
no production-standard FP8 path comparable to Hopper datacenter stacks

name: NVIDIA RTX 6000 Ada Generation
strengths: 48 GB Ada workstation card for larger local inference workloads
strong fit for Triton development when you need more memory than a 4090
constraints: workstation deployment path is less representative than passive server cards
still far below Hopper HBM bandwidth on memory-bound decode

name: GeForce RTX 5090
strengths: much stronger memory bandwidth than Ada consumer cards
Blackwell-era low-precision paths make block-scaled kernels more relevant
worth maintaining dedicated variants rather than reusing Ada launch parameters
constraints: consumer deployment stacks will lag datacenter Blackwell feature availability
kernel portability from Hopper should be validated instead of assumed

name: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Workstation Edition
aliases: nvidia rtx pro 6000 blackwell workstation edition
strengths: 96 GB workstation Blackwell target is excellent for local large-model iteration
bandwidth and VRAM make it a strong bridge between consumer and datacenter deployment
constraints: workstation cooling and deployment assumptions differ from server cards
Blackwell low-precision serving stacks still need validation per framework

name: NVIDIA H100
strengths: excellent FP8 path for transformer inference
very strong HBM bandwidth and tensor core throughput
works well with TensorRT-LLM and serving stacks that lean on paged attention
constraints: still needs per-workload tuning for decode latency and MoE scheduling
small kernels can underutilize the device without graph capture or persistent designs

name: NVIDIA H200
strengths: much higher HBM capacity and bandwidth than H100 for memory-sensitive serving
especially attractive for large-context inference and bigger batch residency
constraints: still needs workload-specific tuning rather than inheriting H100 parameters blindly
availability and deployment coverage are narrower than older Hopper targets

name: NVIDIA B200
strengths: Blackwell datacenter path is the strongest target for block-scaled low-precision kernels
very high HBM bandwidth makes fused attention and grouped GEMM especially attractive
worth specializing kernels and launch configs instead of inheriting Hopper defaults
constraints: tooling and kernel portability are still moving targets across frameworks
new low-precision paths need correctness and calibration checks before deployment

name: NVIDIA L4
strengths: excellent efficiency target for serving workloads
benefits from compact kernels and aggressive memory reduction
constraints: bandwidth is limited relative to high-end datacenter GPUs
decode-heavy models need especially careful KV and quantization choices

name: NVIDIA L40
strengths: 48 GB passive Ada datacenter target with a strong single-GPU inference profile
better fit than L4 when larger weights or KV footprints need more residency
constraints: GDDR bandwidth is still well below Hopper and Blackwell HBM cards
decode can remain memory-bound on larger models

name: NVIDIA L40S
strengths: one of the strongest Ada datacenter serving targets for transformer inference
worth tuning separately from L4 and consumer Ada cards because the workload envelope is different
constraints: still memory-bound relative to Hopper or Blackwell HBM platforms on long-context decode
needs per-workload tuning instead of inheriting 4090 launch parameters

name: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition
aliases: nvidia rtx pro 6000 blackwell server edition
strengths: server-ready Blackwell professional card with large memory and strong low-precision throughput
useful for inference fleets that want Blackwell features outside the HGX class
constraints: less bandwidth than B200-class HBM platforms for the heaviest memory-bound decode cases
serving software coverage is still maturing for newer Blackwell professional SKUs
